comments.author: Prem N.
comments.comment: 1M context is useful, but the failure mode shifts from “model did not see the file” to “model saw too much and weighted the wrong part.” I’d test it with repo-local tasks that require editing across 5-10 files, then measure patch correctness, tool-call recovery, and whether cached context actually lowers end-to-end cost. How are you thinking about evals for long-running coding agents beyond SWE-bench style scores?

comments.author: Sivasankar Natarajan
comments.comment:
Most agent memory bugs I see are really memory routing bugs.

The hard part is not storing more context. It is deciding what gets written, what gets retrieved, and what gets ignored on the next run. A small eval set with “should remember / should forget / should ask again” cases catches more issues than another vector DB tweak.

For builders here: do you test memory as a product behavior, or only as retrieval accuracy?

#AgenticAI

comments.author: Uri Gafni
comments.comment: The hard part is that AI visibility is not a ranking problem, it is a state-space problem: every prompt, product feed, review corpus, and policy constraint changes what the model can safely recommend. I’d be curious how teams are separating signal they can actually move from noise caused by model variance and personalization.

comments.author: Justin Hardy
comments.comment: The real shift is from ranking pages to training retrieval paths. If a product claim is not repeated in crawlable pages, schema, reviews, docs, and trusted third-party mentions, an AI answer has very little reason to surface it. What are teams using as the eval set for “do we show up in buyer questions?”
